poets.name: سعدی (Saadi Shirazi)
poets.bio: Abū-Muhammad Muslih al-Dīn bin Abdallāh Shīrāzī, known as Saadi, was one of the major Persian poets of the medieval period. He is celebrated for the beauty of his style, his moral wisdom, and his deep humanism.

poets.name: مولوی (Rumi)
poets.bio: Jalāl al-Dīn Muḥammad Rūmī, known as Rumi, was a 13th-century Persian poet, Islamic scholar, theologian, and Sufi mystic whose spiritual legacy transcends national and cultural borders.
poets.themes: Mysticism, Divine Love, Unity, Spiritual Rebirth
poets.famous_poem: بشنو این نی چون شکایت می‌کند

poets.name: حافظ (Hafez)
poets.bio: Khwāja Shams-ud-Dīn Muḥammad Ḥāfeẓ-e Shīrāzī, known as Hafez, is the undisputed master of the Persian ghazal. His poetry is celebrated for its lyrical beauty, spiritual depth, and critiques of hypocrisy.
poets.themes: Spiritual Love, Divine Wine, Hypocrisy, Fate
poets.famous_poem: الا یا ایها الساقی ادر کأسا و ناولها

poets.name: خیام (Omar Khayyam)
poets.bio: Omar Khayyam was a Persian polymath, mathematician, astronomer, and philosopher, globally renowned for his Rubaiyat quatrains reflecting on existence, time, and carpe diem.
poets.themes: Existentialism, Transience of Life, Carpe Diem, Wonder
poets.famous_poem: اسرار ازل را نه تو دانی و نه من

poets.name: فردوسی (Ferdowsi)
poets.bio: Hakīm Abu'l-Qāsim Ferdowsi Tusi was the author of the Shahnameh (Book of Kings), the national epic of the Persian-speaking world, which single-handedly revived the Persian language.
poets.themes: Heroism, Patriotism, Epic Tragedy, Wisdom, Justice
poets.famous_poem: به نام خداوند جان و خرد

poets.name: عطار (Attar of Nishapur)
poets.bio: Farid al-Din Attar of Nishapur was a legendary Persian poet and Sufi theoretician whose epic masterpiece Mantiq al-Tayr (Conference of the Birds) describes the soul's journey to the divine.
poets.famous_poem: هفت شهر عشق را عطار گشت

poets.name: نظامی (Nizami Ganjavi)
poets.bio: Nizami Ganjavi is the greatest romantic epic poet in Persian literature, famous for his Khamseh (Quintet), which elevated colloquial romantic drama and philosophical wisdom.
poets.themes: Romantic Tragedy, Courtly Love, Philosophy, Moral Wisdom

poets.name: فروغ (Forough Farrokhzad)
poets.bio: Forough Farrokhzad was an iconic modernist Iranian poet and film director. Writing with bold honesty, she became a pioneer of feminist literature and modern Persian blank verse.
poets.famous_poem: من از نهایت شب حرف می‌زنم

poets.name: سهراب (Sohrab Sepehri)
poets.bio: Sohrab Sepehri was a prominent modern Iranian poet and painter. He developed a unique form of modern blank verse influenced by nature, Zen mysticism, and humanitarian philosophy.
poets.themes: Zen Mysticism, Nature, Simplicity, Quiet Awareness

poets.name: پروین (Parvin Etesami)
poets.bio: Parvin Etesami was one of Iran's most famous female poets. Her poetry employs debate (Monazereh) and fable to deliver powerful critiques of social inequality and moral corruption.
poets.themes: Social Justice, Empathy for the Poor, Moral Debates, Ethics
poets.famous_poem: محتسب مستی به ره دید و گریبانش گرفت

poems.title: بنی آدم اعضای یکدیگرند (Human Beings Are Members of One Another)
poems.translation: A famous poem about human unity and compassion from Saadi's Gulistan.
poems.poetNotes: This poem is from Saadi's Gulistan (Rose Garden), written in 1258 CE. It emphasizes the interconnectedness of all humanity and the importance of empathy.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is inscribed on the United Nations building in New York, symbolizing universal human values. It's often quoted in discussions of human rights and social justice.

poems.title: ای ساربان آهسته ران (O Caravan Leader, Drive Slowly)
poems.translation: An emotional classical ghazal about the departure of the beloved with a caravan.
poems.poetNotes: This ghazal is one of Saadi's most celebrated love poems, expressing the agony of separation using the metaphor of a departing desert caravan.
poems.culturalNotes: The phrase 'ای ساربان' is widely recognized in Persian culture and has been set to music by numerous classical and contemporary Iranian singers.

verses.farsi: ای ساربان آهسته ران کارام جانم می‌رود
verses.translit: Ey sârebân âheste rân kârâme jânam miravad
verses.english: O caravan leader, drive slowly, for the peace of my soul is departing,

phrases.meaning: the peace of my soul / my beloved
walking cypress (metaphor for a tall, graceful beloved)

poems.title: تن آدمی شریف است به جان آدمیت (The Nobility of the Soul)
poems.translation: A didactic poem reflecting on what truly makes a human being noble.
poems.poetNotes: This poem is a prime example of Saadi's moral philosophy, arguing that outward appearance and basic physical desires are shared with animals, while true humanity lies in the soul and virtue.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is frequently taught in schools across the Persian-speaking world to encourage empathy, self-improvement, and ethical living.

poems.title: گلی خوش‌بوی در حمام روزی (The Fragrant Clay of Companionship)
poems.translation: A highly famous didactic story about how companionship shapes character.
poems.poetNotes: This excerpt from the preface of Saadi's Gulistan uses the metaphor of ordinary bath clay absorbing the fragrance of a rose to illustrate the profound influence of good company.
poems.culturalNotes: The phrase 'کمال همنشین در من اثر کرد' (the perfection of my companion influenced me) is one of the most common idioms in the Persian language.

poems.title: سلسله موی دوست حلقه دام بلاست (The Chain of the Friend's Hair)
poems.translation: A beautiful lyrical ghazal about the sweet suffering of love and devotion.
poems.poetNotes: In this lyric, Saadi reflects on how love binds a person, yet this binding is preferred to freedom. It showcases his signature style: simple words carrying profound emotional weight.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem represents the classic thematic trope of the beloved's hair acting as a snare or chain for the lover's heart, a central motif in classical Persian poetry.

poems.title: بشنو این نی چون شکایت می‌کند (The Song of the Reed)

poems.poetNotes: Rumi's Masnavi opens not with a traditional praise of God, but with the 'Song of the Reed', which represents the human soul lamenting its exile from its spiritual source.
poems.culturalNotes: The ney (reed flute) is the core instrument of Sufi ceremony and symbolizes the hollowed-out soul through which divine breath flows.

poems.title: بازآ بازآ هر آنچه هستی بازآ (Come Back, Whoever You Are)
poems.translation: The world-famous quatrain of spiritual invitation and unconditional grace.
poems.poetNotes: Though historically attributed by some to Abu Sa'id Abul-Khayr, this rubai is globally recognized under Rumi's legacy, embodying the open, universalist ethos of Sufism.
poems.culturalNotes: This verse is a cornerstone of the mystical notion of 'non-judgment' and spiritual homecoming in Persian and global culture.

verses.farsi: بازآ بازآ هر آنچه هستی بازآ
verses.translit: Bâz-â bâz-â har ânche hasti bâz-â
verses.english: Come back, come back, whoever you are, come back!

verses.farsi: گر کافر و گبر و بت‌پرستی بازآ
verses.translit: Gar kâfar o gabr o bot-parasti bâz-â
verses.english: Even if you are an unbeliever, a pagan, or a fire-worshipper, come back!

verses.farsi: این درگه ما درگه نومیدی نیست
verses.translit: In dargehe mâ dargehe nomidi nist
verses.english: This threshold of ours is not the portal of despair.

verses.farsi: صد بار اگر توبه شکستی بازآ
verses.translit: Sad bâr agar tob-e shekasti bâz-â
verses.english: Even if you have broken your vows a hundred times, come back!

poems.title: بنمای رخ که باغ و گلستانم آرزوست (Show Your Face)
poems.translation: A passionate ghazal of divine longing, searching for the ultimate human essence.
poems.poetNotes: Written in Rumi's Diwan-e Shams, this poem describes the spiritual searcher's weariness with beasts and monsters, seeking the light of the divine human face instead.
poems.culturalNotes: The verse 'انسانم آرزوست' (I yearn for a true human) is a famous proverb in Iran, expressing a desire for genuine humanity and integrity.

verses.farsi: بنمای رخ که باغ و گلستانم آرزوست
verses.translit: Benmây rokh ke bâgh o golestânam ârezust
verses.english: Show your face, for I long for the garden and the meadow;

phrases.meaning: a human is my desire / I long for a human

verses.farsi: بگشای لب که قند فراوانم آرزوست

poems.title: مرده بدم زنده شدم (I Was Dead, I Became Alive)
poems.translation: An ecstatic ghazal describing Rumi's total transformation through love.
poems.poetNotes: This poem records the emotional and spiritual rebirth Rumi experienced after meeting Shams of Tabriz. It utilizes contrasting states (dead/alive, weeping/laughing) to capture mystical ecstasy.
poems.culturalNotes: The dynamic rhythm of this ghazal mimics the heartbeat of the Sama dance (whirling dervishes), moving from slow reflection to ecstatic joy.

poems.title: یوسف گمگشته بازآید به کنعان غم مخور (Lost Joseph)
poems.translation: An inspiring ghazal of optimism, hope, and cosmic reassurance.
poems.poetNotes: This poem is a classic of Hafez's consoling style. He uses the Quranic/Biblical story of Joseph returning to Jacob to assure the reader that joy will return after tragedy.

poems.title: مژده ای دل که مسیحا نفسی می‌آید (Breath of Messiah)
poems.translation: A beautiful ghazal announcing the arrival of a divine healer.
poems.poetNotes: In this lyric, Hafez employs the Islamic metaphor of Jesus's breath (which could raise the dead) to describe the arrival of the beloved who heals the lover's broken spirit.
poems.culturalNotes: The term 'مسیحانفس' is a common poetic idiom in Persian for someone whose words or presence brings immediate life, hope, and comfort.

verses.english: For in that mirror, they gave me news of the manifestation of the Divine Essence.

phrases.meaning: water of life / elixir of immortality

verses.farsi: من اگر کامروا گشتم و خوش‌دل چه عجب
verses.translit: Man agar kâmravâ gashtam o khoshdel che ajab
verses.english: If I am now successful and happy, what wonder is there?

phrases.meaning: water of life / elixir of immortality

verses.farsi: مستحق بودم و این‌ها به زکاتم دادند
verses.translit: Mostahagh budam o inhâ be zakâtam dâdand
verses.english: I was deserving, and they gave me these blessings as spiritual charity.

phrases.meaning: water of life / elixir of immortality

poems.title: اسرار ازل را نه تو دانی و نه من (Secrets of Eternity)
poems.translation: An existential quatrain about the limits of human knowledge regarding eternity.
poems.poetNotes: Khayyam uses the metaphor of a theatrical veil. Humanity struggles to see what is behind it, but once the veil of life falls, both the seeker and the mystery vanish.
poems.culturalNotes: This rubai is a classic of Persian agnostic/skeptical philosophy, emphasizing humility and acceptance of human cognitive limitations.

verses.farsi: اسرار ازل را نه تو دانی و نه من
verses.translit: Asrâre azal râ na to dâni va na man
verses.english: The secrets of eternity are known neither to you nor to me,

phrases.meaning: riddle words / text of the puzzle

verses.farsi: وین حرف معما نه تو خوانی و نه من
verses.translit: Vin harfe mo'ammâ na to khvâni va na man
verses.english: And this riddle's text can be read neither by you nor by me.

phrases.meaning: riddle words / text of the puzzle

verses.farsi: هست از پس پرده گفتگوی من و تو
verses.translit: Hast az pase parde goftoguye man o to
verses.english: There is talk of you and me behind the veil,

phrases.meaning: riddle words / text of the puzzle

verses.farsi: چون پرده برافتد نه تو مانی و نه من
verses.translit: Chon parde bar-oftad na to mâni va na man
verses.english: But when the veil drops, neither you nor I shall remain.

phrases.meaning: riddle words / text of the puzzle

poems.title: این کوزه چو من عاشق زاری بوده است (The Clay Cup)
poems.translation: A melancholic quatrain on the transience of life and the cyclic nature of clay.
poems.poetNotes: Khayyam frequently uses the pottery wheel as a metaphor. The clay of the cup was once a breathing lover, and its handle was an arm around a beloved's neck.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a powerful expression of 'Memento Mori' in Persian literature, urging the reader to appreciate the physical world before returning to dust.

verses.farsi: این کوزه چو من عاشق زاری بوده است
verses.translit: In kuze cho man âsheghe zâri bude ast
verses.english: This clay pot was once a weeping lover like me,

verses.farsi: در بند رخ نکوی یاری بوده است
verses.translit: Dar bande rokhe nokuye yâri bude ast
verses.english: Entrapped by the beautiful face of a beloved.

verses.farsi: این دسته که بر گردن او می‌بینی
verses.translit: In daste ke bar gardene u mibini
verses.english: This handle which you see upon its neck

verses.farsi: دستی است که بر گردن یاری بوده است
verses.translit: Dasti ast ke bar gardene yâri bude ast
verses.english: Is an arm that was once wrapped around a beloved's neck.

poems.title: این قافله عمر عجب می‌گذرد (The Caravan of Life)
poems.translation: The ultimate Carpe Diem quatrain about the fast-moving caravan of life.
poems.poetNotes: Khayyam portrays lifetime as a fast, unstoppable desert caravan. Since it passes so quickly, he advises us to seize the present moment of joy.
poems.culturalNotes: The phrase 'عجب می‌گذرد' (how strangely it passes) is a common exclamation of existential wonder in modern Iranian conversation.

verses.farsi: این قافله عمر عجب می‌گذرد
verses.translit: In ghâfeleye omr ajab migozarad
verses.english: This caravan of life passes in a strange, wondrous way;

verses.farsi: دریاب دمی که با طرب می‌گذرد
verses.translit: Daryâb dami ke bâ tarab migozarad
verses.english: Seize the moment that is passing in joy.

verses.farsi: ساقی غم فردای حریفان چه خوری
verses.translit: Sâghi ghame fardâye harifân che khori
verses.english: Cupbearer, why do you worry about the tomorrow of rivals?

verses.farsi: پیش آر پیاله را که شب می‌گذرد
verses.translit: Pish âr piyâle râ ke shab migozarad
verses.english: Bring forward the goblet, for the night is slipping away.

poems.title: خیام اگر ز باده مستی خوش باش (Be Happy)
poems.translation: A philosophical quatrain about existence, non-existence, and joy.
poems.poetNotes: Khayyam matches the core philosophical concepts of 'being' and 'not-being'. He argues that since our end is non-existence, we should treat our present existence as a bonus of joy.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a great representation of the Epicurean strand of Persian literature, emphasizing joy over religious dogmatism.

verses.farsi: خیام اگر ز باده مستی خوش باش
verses.translit: Khayyâm agar ze bâde masti khosh bâsh
verses.english: Khayyam, if you are drunk on wine, be happy!

verses.farsi: با ماه رخی اگر نشستی خوش باش
verses.translit: Bâ mâh-rokhi agar neshasti khosh bâsh
verses.english: If you are sitting with a moon-faced beauty, be happy!

verses.farsi: چون عاقبت کار جهان نیستی است
verses.translit: Chon âghebate kâre jahân nisti ast
verses.english: Since the ultimate end of the world is non-existence,

verses.farsi: انگار که نیستی چو هستی خوش باش
verses.translit: Engâr ke nisti cho hasti khosh bâsh
verses.english: Imagine you already do not exist; as long as you do, be happy!

poems.title: گویند کسان بهشت با حور خوش است (Cash vs Credit of Life)
poems.translation: A witty rubai comparing the promised future heaven with the concrete present.
poems.poetNotes: Here Khayyam makes his famous defense of material experience over religious promises. He advises taking the 'cash' of this world rather than waiting for the 'credit' of heaven.
poems.culturalNotes: The line 'کآواز دهل شنیدن از دور خوش است' (for the sound of the drum is sweet only from a distance) is one of the most famous proverbs in the Persian language.

verses.farsi: گویند کسان بهشت با حور خوش است
verses.translit: Guyand kasân behesht bâ hur khosh ast
verses.english: Some say that paradise with beautiful maidens is sweet,

phrases.meaning: let go / hold off / withdraw hand

verses.farsi: من می‌گویم که آب انگور خوش است
verses.translit: Man miguyam ke âbe angur khosh ast
verses.english: I say that the juice of the grape is sweet.

phrases.meaning: let go / hold off / withdraw hand

verses.farsi: این نقد بگیر و دست از آن نسیه بدار
verses.translit: In naghd beghir o dast az ân nesye bedâr
verses.english: Take this cash of the present, and let go of that credit,

phrases.meaning: let go / hold off / withdraw hand

verses.farsi: کاواز دهل شنیدن از دور خوش است
verses.translit: K'âvâze dohol shenidan az dur khosh ast
verses.english: For the beating of the drum sounds sweet only from afar.

words.meaning: for the sound of (contraction of که آواز)

phrases.meaning: let go / hold off / withdraw hand

poems.title: به نام خداوند جان و خرد (In the Name of the Lord of Soul and Wisdom)
poems.translation: The majestic opening sequence of the Shahnameh, the national epic of Greater Iran.
poems.poetNotes: Ferdowsi's opening focuses on God as the creator of 'Soul' and 'Intellect/Wisdom', placing wisdom at the very center of his epic worldview rather than religious dogma.
poems.culturalNotes: This is perhaps the single most famous opening couplet in Persian literature. Every Iranian student learns this line in elementary school.

verses.farsi: به نام خداوند جان و خرد
verses.translit: Be nâme khodâvande jân o kherad
verses.english: In the name of the Lord of soul and wisdom,

verses.farsi: کزین برتر اندیشه برنگذرد
verses.translit: K'azin bartar andishe bar-nagzard
verses.english: Than which no higher thought can pass.

words.meaning: that from this (contraction of که از این)

verses.farsi: خداوند نام و خداوند جای
verses.translit: Khodâvande nâm o khodâvande jây
verses.english: The Lord of renown and the Lord of place,

verses.farsi: خداوند روزی‌ده رهنمای
verses.translit: Khodâvande ruzi-deh rahnemây
verses.english: The Giver of daily bread and the Guide.

verses.farsi: خداوند کیوان و گردان سپهر
verses.translit: Khodâvande keyvân o gardâne sepehr
verses.english: The Lord of Saturn and the rotating heavens,

verses.farsi: فروزنده ماه و ناهید و مهر
verses.translit: Foruzandeye mâh o Nâhid o mehr
verses.english: The Illuminator of the Moon, Venus, and the Sun.

verses.farsi: ز نام و نشان و گمان برتر است
verses.translit: Ze nâmo neshâno gomân bartar ast
verses.english: He is far above name, sign, and imagination;

verses.farsi: نگارنده برشده پیکر است
verses.translit: Negârandeye bar-shodey peykar ast
verses.english: He is the Designer of the exalted constellations.

verses.farsi: به بینندگان آفریننده را
verses.translit: Be binandegân âfarinandeye râ
verses.english: With these physical eyes, the Creator

verses.farsi: نبینی مرنجان دو بیننده را
verses.translit: Nabi'ni maranjân do binandeye râ
verses.english: You cannot see; do not trouble those two eyes.

words.meaning: do not trouble! / do not hurt!

verses.farsi: نیابد بدو نیز اندیشه راه
verses.translit: Nayâbad bedo niz andishe râh
verses.english: Intellect itself cannot find a path to Him,

verses.farsi: که او برتر از نام و از جایگاه
verses.translit: Ke u bartar az nâmo az jâygâh
verses.english: For He is far beyond name and location.

poems.title: توانا بود هر که دانا بود (Knowledge is Power)
poems.translation: Ferdowsi's famous philosophical dissertation on the power of wisdom.
poems.poetNotes: This excerpt highlights Ferdowsi's rationalist outlook. Wisdom acts as the guide of the soul in both worlds, protecting humans from fear and injustice.
poems.culturalNotes: The line 'توانا بود هر که دانا بود' is the ultimate educational slogan in modern Iran, similar to the western proverb 'Knowledge is Power'.

verses.farsi: خرد رهنمای و خرد دلگشای
verses.translit: Kherad rahnemây o kherad delgoshây
verses.english: Wisdom is the guide, and wisdom is the opener of hearts;

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: خرد دست گیرد به هر دو سرای
verses.translit: Kherad dast ghirad be har do sarây
verses.english: Wisdom holds your hand in both worlds.

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: خرد تیره و خرد شادمان
verses.translit: Kherad tire va kherad shâdmân
verses.english: Wisdom makes you grave, and wisdom makes you joyful;

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: خرد باد جان تو را پاسبان
verses.translit: Kherad bâd jâne to râ pâsebân
verses.english: May wisdom be the protector of your soul.

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: نخستین نشان خرد آن بود
verses.translit: Nakhostin neshâne kherad ân bovad
verses.english: The very first sign of wisdom is this,

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: که از بد به گیتی هراسان بود
verses.translit: Ke az bad be ghiti harâsân bovad
verses.english: That one fears and retreats from evil in the world.

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: توانا بود هر که دانا بود
verses.translit: Tavânâ bovad har ke dânâ bovad
verses.english: Capable and powerful is he who is wise,

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: ز دانش دل پیر برنا بود
verses.translit: Ze dânesh dele pir bornâ bovad
verses.english: And through knowledge, the heart of the old man grows young.

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: از این پرده برتر سخن‌گاه نیست
verses.translit: Az in parde bartar sokhan-gâh nist
verses.english: There is no higher stage for speech than this theme,

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

verses.farsi: به هستیش اندیشه را راه نیست
verses.translit: Be hastiyash andishe râ râh nist
verses.english: And thought has no path to God's ultimate essence.

phrases.meaning: heart of the old / elderly heart

poems.title: یکی داستانست پر آب چشم (A Tale Full of Tears)
poems.translation: The tragic prologue introducing the battle of Rostam and Sohrab.
poems.poetNotes: Ferdowsi introduces the duel between father and son with a poetic warning on the cruelty of fate. He compares death to a harsh wind that blows down unripe fruit.
poems.culturalNotes: The tragedy of Rostam and Sohrab is the most famous narrative in the Shahnameh, exemplifying the literary concept of dramatic irony.

verses.farsi: یکی داستانست پر آب چشم
verses.translit: Yaki dâstânast pore âbe chashm
verses.english: Here is a tale full of tears,

verses.farsi: دل نازک از رستم آید به خشم
verses.translit: Dele nâzok az Rostam âyad be khashm
verses.english: A tender heart will grow angry with Rostam.

verses.farsi: اگر تندبادی برآید ز کنج
verses.translit: Agar tond-bâdi bar-âyad ze konj
verses.english: If a violent wind blows from a corner,

verses.farsi: به خاک افکند نارسیده ترنج
verses.translit: Be khâk afkanad nâreside toronj
verses.english: And casts down an unripe orange to the ground,

verses.farsi: نه دانی که آن باد با او چه کرد
verses.translit: Na dâni ke ân bâd bâ u che kard
verses.english: Do you not know what that wind did to it,

verses.farsi: که از شاخ بی وقت او را ببرد
verses.translit: Ke az shâkhe bi-vaght u râ bobord
verses.english: That it cut it off from the branch before its time?

verses.farsi: به بازی‌گری‌های چرخ بلند
verses.translit: Be bâzigharihâye charkhe boland
verses.english: Look at the playful games of the high heavens,

verses.farsi: نگاه کن که دارد به ما بس گزند
verses.translit: Negâh kon ke dârad be mâ bas gazand
verses.english: Which hold so much harm and cruelty for us.

verses.farsi: چنین است رسم سرای درشت
verses.translit: Chonin ast rasme sarâye dorosht
verses.english: Such is the custom of this rough dwelling-place (the world):

verses.farsi: یکی را نوازد یکی را بکشت
verses.translit: Yaki râ navâzad yaki râ bokosht
verses.english: It caresses one, and slays another.

words.meaning: slew / killed (archaic past singular of کشتن)

poems.title: چو ایران نباشد تن من مباد (For the Love of Iran)
poems.translation: The ultimate patriotic epic excerpt expressing total devotion to the land.
poems.poetNotes: This passage highlights the collective resolve of the epic heroes. It claims that death is far better than seeing the country occupied by foreign enemies.
poems.culturalNotes: These lines are the absolute peak of Iranian national sentiment, quoted in music, speeches, and patriotic gatherings.

verses.farsi: دریغ است ایران که ویران شود
verses.translit: Darigh ast Irân ke virân shavad
verses.english: It is a tragedy that Iran should be ruined,

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: کنام پلنگان و شیران شود
verses.translit: Konâme palangân o shirân shavad
verses.english: And become the lair of leopards and lions.

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: چو ایران نباشد تن من مباد
verses.translit: Cho Irân nabâshad tane man mobâd
verses.english: If Iran is not to exist, let my body cease to be,

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: بدین بوم و بر زنده یک تن مباد
verses.translit: Bedin bumo bar zende yek tan mobâd
verses.english: May not a single soul remain alive in this land.

words.meaning: to this / in this (contraction of به این)

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: همه روی جنگی به کار آوریم
verses.translit: Hame ruye jangy be kâr âverim
verses.english: Let us all bring our warrior faces to the battlefield,

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: از آن پس جهان تنگ‌تار آوریم
verses.translit: Az ân pas jahân tang-târ âverim
verses.english: And make the world narrow and dark for our foes.

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: اگر سر به سر تن به کشتن دهیم
verses.translit: Agar sar be sar tan be koshtan dahim
verses.english: Even if we all, from first to last, give our bodies to death,

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: از آن به که کشور به دشمن دهیم
verses.translit: Az ân beh ke keshvar be doshman dahim
verses.english: It is far better than handing our country to the enemy.

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: بیا تا همه روی به جنگ آوریم
verses.translit: Biyâ tâ hame ruy be jang âverim
verses.english: Come, let us all turn our faces to battle,

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

verses.farsi: به دشمن جهان سخت و تنگ آوریم
verses.translit: Be doshman jahân sakht o tang âverim
verses.english: And make the world harsh and narrow for the enemy.

phrases.meaning: may my body not exist / let me perish

poems.title: بسی رنج بردم در این سال سی (Immortality of the Epic)
poems.translation: Ferdowsi's proud reflection on his thirty-year struggle to write the epic.
poems.poetNotes: At the end of the Shahnameh, Ferdowsi reflects on his achievement. He notes that while normal brick buildings decay, his 'palace of verse' is immune to the winds of time.
poems.culturalNotes: The phrase 'عجم زنده کردم بدین پارسی' (I revived the Persian identity with this Persian language) highlights Ferdowsi's role as the savior of the Persian language under Arab rule.

verses.farsi: بسی رنج بردم در این سال سی
verses.translit: Basi ranj bordam dar in sâle si
verses.english: Great pain I suffered during these thirty years,

words.meaning: I carried / I suffered (past singular of بردن)

verses.farsi: عجم زنده کردم بدین پارسی
verses.translit: Ajam zende kardam bedin Pârsi
verses.english: But I revived the Persian identity through this Persian tongue.

words.meaning: to this / in this (contraction of به این)

verses.farsi: پی افکندم از نظم کاخی بلند
verses.translit: Pey afkandam az nazm kâkhi boland
verses.english: I have cast the foundation of a tall palace of verse,

words.meaning: I cast / threw (past singular of افکندن)

verses.farsi: که از باد و باران نیابد گزند
verses.translit: Ke az bâd o bârân nayâbad gazand
verses.english: Which shall suffer no harm from wind and rain.

verses.farsi: بناهای آباد گردد خراب
verses.translit: Banâhâye âbâd gardad kharâb
verses.english: Magnificent buildings will eventually fall to ruin,

verses.farsi: ز باران و از تابش آفتاب
verses.translit: Ze bârân o az tâbishe âftâb
verses.english: From rain and the burning rays of the sun.

verses.farsi: نمیرم از این پس که من زنده‌ام
verses.translit: Namiram az in pas ke man zende'am
verses.english: I shall never die from now on, for I am alive,

verses.farsi: که تخم سخن را پراکنده‌ام
verses.translit: Ke tokhme sokhan râ parâkande'am
verses.english: Since I have scattered the seeds of this language.

verses.farsi: هر آن کس که دارد هش و رای و دین
verses.translit: Har ân kas ke dârad hosh o râyo din
verses.english: Whoever possesses intelligence, wisdom, and faith,

verses.farsi: پس از مرگ بر من کند آفرین
verses.translit: Pas az marg bar man konad âfarin
verses.english: Will send blessings upon me after my death.

poems.title: هست ما را پادشاهی بی‌خلاف (We Have a King)
poems.translation: The opening quest sequence from the Conference of the Birds.
poems.poetNotes: Attar introduces the birds of the world gathering to choose a king. The hoopoe tells them that their true king, the Simurgh, lives behind the mystical Mount Qaf.
poems.culturalNotes: The Simurgh represents the divine essence, and the birds' journey represents the human soul's path to enlightenment.

verses.farsi: آفرین جان‌آفرین پاک را
verses.translit: Âfarin jân-âfarine pâk râ
verses.english: Blessings upon the holy Creator of the soul,

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: آن که جان بخشید مشتی خاک را
verses.translit: Ân ke jân bakhshid moshti khâk râ
verses.english: He who gave life to a handful of dust.

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: هست ما را پادشاهی بی‌خلاف
verses.translit: Hast mâ râ pâdeshâhi bi-khelâf
verses.english: We have a King indeed without dispute,

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: در پس کوهی که هست آن کوه قاف
verses.translit: Dar pase kuhi ke hast ân kuhe Ghâf
verses.english: Behind a mountain which is called Mount Qaf.

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: نام او سیمرغ سلطان جهان
verses.translit: Nâme u Simorgh soltâne jahân
verses.english: His name is Simurgh, the Sovereign of the universe;

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: او به ما نزدیک و ما از وی نهان
verses.translit: U be mâ nazdiko mâ az vey nahân
verses.english: He is close to us, and yet we are hidden from Him.

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: در حریم عز او آرام نیست
verses.translit: Dar harime ezze u ârâm nist
verses.english: In His court of glory, there is no rest,

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: زان که آن جا هیچ کس را نام نیست
verses.translit: Zân ke ân jâ hich kas râ nâm nist
verses.english: For in that place, no one retains an individual identity.

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: صد هزاران خلق سرگردان اوست
verses.translit: Sad hezârân khalghe sergardâne ust
verses.english: Hundreds of thousands of creatures are wandering lost in search of Him,

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

verses.farsi: در طلب سرگشته و حیران اوست
verses.translit: Dar talab sargashte va heyrâne ust
verses.english: Struggling and bewildered in their quest for Him.

phrases.meaning: Mount Qaf (mythological boundary of the world)
wanders looking for Him / is lost in Him

poems.title: گفت ما را هفت وادی در ره است (The Seven Valleys)
poems.translation: The famous description of the Seven Valleys of the spiritual path.
poems.poetNotes: This passage lists the seven stages the birds must cross to reach the Simurgh: Search, Love, Knowledge, Independence, Unity, Bewilderment, and Poverty/Annihilation.
poems.culturalNotes: The 'Seven Valleys' (هفت وادی) is a foundational concept in Sufi psychology, representing the progression of the human heart towards divine union.

verses.farsi: گفت ما را هفت وادی در ره است
verses.translit: Goft mâ râ haft vâdi dar rah ast
verses.english: He said: 'Seven Valleys lie ahead on our path,

verses.farsi: چون گذشتی زان سپس منزلگه است
verses.translit: Chon gozashti zân sepas menzilgah ast
verses.english: Only when you cross them will you reach the final resting place.

verses.farsi: اولت وادی طلب آغاز کار
verses.translit: Avvalat vâdiye talab âghâze kâr
verses.english: First is the Valley of Search, the beginning of the work;

verses.farsi: وادیت دوم عشق آمد بی‌کنار
verses.translit: Vâdiyat dovvom eshgh âmad bi-kenâr
verses.english: Second is the Valley of Love, which has no shore.

verses.farsi: وادی سوم معرفت را جایگاه
verses.translit: Vâdiye sevvom ma'refat râ jâygâh
verses.english: The third Valley is the seat of Knowledge;

verses.farsi: وادی چهارم استغنا به راه
verses.translit: Vâdiye chahârom estighnâ be râh
verses.english: The fourth Valley on the road is Independence and Detachment.

verses.farsi: وادی پنجم توحید پاک
verses.translit: Vâdiye panjom touhide pâk
verses.english: The fifth Valley is pure Unity;

verses.farsi: وادی ششم حیرت دردناک
verses.translit: Vâdiye sheshom heyrate dardnâk
verses.english: The sixth Valley is painful Bewilderment.

verses.farsi: وادی هفتم فقر است و فنا
verses.translit: Vâdiye haftom faghr ast o fanâ
verses.english: The seventh Valley is Poverty and Annihilation,

verses.farsi: بعد از این دیگر کجا یابی بقا
verses.translit: Ba'd az in digar kojâ yâbi baghâ
verses.english: After this, where else can you find eternal life?'

poems.title: یک شبی پروانگان جمع آمدند (The Moths and the Candle)
poems.translation: The allegory of the moths seeking the reality of the candle.
poems.poetNotes: Attar tells a fable of moths who want to know about the candle. Two return with descriptions, but the elder says only the third moth—who flies directly into the flame—truly knows.
poems.culturalNotes: The moth and the candle (شمع و پروانه) is the supreme Persian metaphor for the lover sacrificing his ego to be united with the light of the Beloved.

verses.farsi: یک شبی پروانگان جمع آمدند
verses.translit: Yek shabi parvânegân jam' âmadand
verses.english: One night, the moths gathered together,

verses.farsi: در مضیقی طالب شمع آمدند
verses.translit: Dar mozighi tâlebe shama' âmadand
verses.english: In a narrow chamber, seeking the candle.

verses.farsi: جمله می‌گفتند می‌باید یکی
verses.translit: Jomle migoftand mibâyad yaki
verses.english: They all said: 'There must be one among us

verses.farsi: کو خبر آرد ز شمعمان اندکی
verses.translit: Ku khabar ârad ze sham'amân andaki
verses.english: Who can bring us some news of our candle.'

words.meaning: who / that he (contraction of که او)

verses.farsi: شد یکی پروانه تا قصر وصال
verses.translit: Shod yaki parvâne tâ ghasre vesâl
verses.english: One moth set off to the palace of union,

verses.farsi: دید اندر نور شمع آن سرخ بال
verses.translit: Did andar nure sham' ân sorkh bâl
verses.english: And saw that glowing wing inside the candle's light.

verses.farsi: او ز شمع آمد خبر داد از جمال
verses.translit: U ze sham' âmad khabar dâd az jamâl
verses.english: He returned from the candle and gave news of its beauty,

verses.farsi: گفت این است نشان نور و کمال
verses.translit: Goft in ast neshâne nuro kamâl
verses.english: Saying: 'This is the sign of light and perfection.'

verses.farsi: گفت پیر مصلحت را جان من
verses.translit: Goft pire maslahat râ jâne man
verses.english: The wise elder said: 'O my dear soul,

verses.farsi: این نشانی نیست از جانان من
verses.translit: In neshâni nist az jânâne man
verses.english: This is no real trace of my Beloved.'

poems.title: گم شدم در خود چنان کز خویش ناپیدا شدم (Lost in Love)
poems.translation: A beautiful lyrical ghazal on losing oneself in the divine ocean.
poems.poetNotes: This ghazal focuses on the concept of 'Fana' (ego-loss). Attar claims that by dissolving his individual self, he became united with the vast ocean of divinity.
poems.culturalNotes: The dewdrop and the ocean metaphor ('شبنمی بودم ز دریا غرق در دریا شدم') is a key image in Eastern mysticism for the dissolution of the ego.

verses.farsi: گم شدم در خود چنان کز خویش ناپیدا شدم
verses.translit: Gom shodam dar khod chonân kaz khish nâpeydâ shodam
verses.english: I became so lost in myself that I vanished from my own sight,

words.meaning: who from / that from (contraction of که از)

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: شبنمی بودم ز دریا غرق در دریا شدم
verses.translit: Shabnami budam ze daryâ ghargh dar daryâ shodam
verses.english: I was a dewdrop from the ocean, and I became drowned in the ocean.

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: هر که در عشق تو مخروبه شد از دست رفت
verses.translit: Har ke dar eshghe to makhroube shod az dast raft
verses.english: Whoever becomes ruined in your love is lost to the world,

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: چون به عشق تو رسیدم مست و بی‌پروا شدم
verses.translit: Chon be eshghe to rasidam mast o bi-parvâ shodam
verses.english: When I attained your love, I became intoxicated and reckless.

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: جان ز من بگسست و تن بی‌خویش در کار تو شد
verses.translit: Jân ze man bogsast o tan bi-khish dar kâre to shod
verses.english: My soul broke away from me, and my body became mindless in your service,

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: تا ز بند خویشتن رستم عجب شیدا شدم
verses.translit: Tâ ze bande khishtan rostam ajab sheydâ shodam
verses.english: Once I escaped the shackles of self, how wondrously mad I became.

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: چون دل من محرم اسرار غیب عشق گشت
verses.translit: Chon dele man mahrame asrâre gheybe eshgh gasht
verses.english: When my heart became a confidant of the unseen secrets of love,

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

verses.farsi: در میان جمع مستان سرور و پیدا شدم
verses.translit: Dar miâne jam'e mastân sarvar o peydâ shodam
verses.english: I became a leader and visible among the gathering of the drunkards.

phrases.meaning: shackles of the ego / bonds of self
secrets of the unseen / divine mysteries

poems.title: بعد از این وادی فقر است و فنا (The Valley of Poverty & Annihilation)
poems.translation: The final stage of the bird's journey representing ego dissolution.
poems.poetNotes: This excerpt details the Seventh Valley. Attar claims that in this stage, the seeker is completely silenced, and their individual self is absorbed like a shadow in the sun.
poems.culturalNotes: The description of this valley is highly mystical, using paradoxes to show that true spiritual existence only begins after the death of the ego.

verses.farsi: بعد از این وادی فقر است و فنا
verses.translit: Ba'd az in vâdiye faghr ast o fanâ
verses.english: Next lies the Valley of Poverty and Annihilation,

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: کی بود اینجا سخن گفتن روا
verses.translit: Key bovad injâ sokhan goftan ravâ
verses.english: When is it ever fitting to speak words here?

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: عین این وادی فراموشی بود
verses.translit: Eyne in vâdi farâmushi bovad
verses.english: The very essence of this valley is forgetfulness,

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: گنگی و کری و بیهوشی بود
verses.translit: Gongi o karri o bihushi bovad
verses.english: It is dumbness, deafness, and unconsciousness.

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: صد هزاران سایه جاوید تو
verses.translit: Sad hezârân sâyeye jâvide to
verses.english: Hundreds of thousands of your eternal shadows

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: گم شود در یک شعاع شید تو
verses.translit: Gom shavad dar yek sho'â'e shide to
verses.english: Will vanish in a single ray of your blazing sun.

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: هر که در دریا فنا شد سر به سر
verses.translit: Har ke dar daryâ fanâ shod sar-be-sar
verses.english: Whoever is completely dissolved in this ocean,

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

verses.farsi: او دگر هرگز نیابد زان اثر
verses.translit: U degar hargez nayâbad zân asar
verses.english: Will never again leave any trace of themselves behind.

phrases.meaning: the very essence of this valley

poems.title: ای نام تو بهترین سرآغاز (O Name of Yours, the Best Beginning)
poems.translation: The beautiful opening prayer of Layla and Majnun.
poems.poetNotes: Nizami begins his tragic romance of Layla and Majnun with a powerful monotheistic prayer. He declares God's name as the key to all secrets and stories.
poems.culturalNotes: This couplet is highly popular. Books, essays, and classrooms in Iran often begin by writing this verse on the board as a blessing.

verses.farsi: ای نام تو بهترین سرآغاز
verses.translit: Ey nâme to behtarin sar-âghâz
verses.english: O Name of Yours, the best beginning!

verses.farsi: بی‌نام تو نامه کی کنم باز
verses.translit: Bi-nâme to nâme key konam bâz
verses.english: Without your name, how could I ever open this book?

verses.farsi: ای یاد تو مونس روانم
verses.translit: Ey yâde to monese ravânam
verses.english: O remembrance of You, the companion of my soul!

words.meaning: my soul / my life / my walking

verses.farsi: جز نام تو نیست بر زبانم
verses.translit: Joz nâme to nist bar zabânam
verses.english: There is nothing on my tongue except Your name.

verses.farsi: ای کارگشای هر رمیده
verses.translit: Ey kâr-goshâye har ramide
verses.english: O Solver of the problems of every wanderer,

words.meaning: solver of problems / opener of works

verses.farsi: سرچشمه رحمت رسیده
verses.translit: Sar-cheshmeye rahmate raside
verses.english: The source of ultimate mercy which has arrived.

verses.farsi: سازنده کار هر بنده
verses.translit: Sâzandeye kâre har bande
verses.english: The Arranger of the affairs of every servant,

verses.farsi: دارنده چرخ شتابنده
verses.translit: Dârandeye charkhe shetâbande
verses.english: The Upholder of the fast-turning heavens.

verses.farsi: در دل ز غمت چراغ دارم
verses.translit: Dar del ze ghamat cherâgh dâram
verses.english: In my heart, I keep a lamp lit from your grief,

verses.farsi: در سر ز هوای باغ دارم
verses.translit: Dar sar ze havâye bâgh dâram
verses.english: And in my head, I hold the desire for Your garden.

poems.title: چو شب بنشست فرهاد بلاکش (Farhad the Mountain Carver)
poems.translation: The tragic scene of Farhad carving Mount Behistun out of love.
poems.poetNotes: Nizami describes the lover Farhad, who was challenged by King Khosrow to carve a passage through a mountain. Out of love for Shirin, Farhad works tirelessly, his tears turning to blood.
poems.culturalNotes: Farhad is the ultimate symbol of the 'laborer-lover' who performs impossible physical feats of dedication and dies of heartbreak.

verses.farsi: چو شب بنشست فرهاد بلاکش
verses.translit: Cho shab benshast Farhâde balâ-kash
verses.english: When night fell upon the suffering Farhad,

words.meaning: sat down (archaic past singular of نشستن)

verses.farsi: به یاد لعل شیرین با دلی خوش
verses.translit: Be yâde la'le Shirin bâ deli khosh
verses.english: With a happy heart, thinking of the ruby lips of Shirin,

verses.farsi: گرفت آن تیشه بی‌رحم در دست
verses.translit: Gereft ân tisheye bi-rahm dar dast
verses.english: He took that merciless axe in hand,

verses.farsi: به ضرب کوه خارا بند بشکست
verses.translit: Be zarbe kuhe khârâ band beshkest
verses.english: And with blows, broke the barriers of the granite mountain.

verses.farsi: به هر ضربت که بر سنگ آوریدی
verses.translit: Be har zarbat ke bar sang âvaridi
verses.english: With every blow that he struck upon the stone,

verses.farsi: ز سنگ خارا خون دل چکیدی
verses.translit: Ze sange khârâ khune del chakidi
verses.english: The blood of his heart dripped from the granite rock.

verses.farsi: صدای تیشه او در کوهساران
verses.translit: Sedâye tisheye u dar kuhsârân
verses.english: The sound of his pickaxe in the mountains

verses.farsi: طنین‌انداز شد چون جویباران
verses.translit: Tanin-andâz shod chon juybârân
verses.english: Echoed around like flowing streams.

verses.farsi: بگفتا عشق شیرین است کارم
verses.translit: Bageftâ eshghe Shirin ast kâram
verses.english: He said: 'The love of Shirin is my work,

verses.farsi: به غیر از جان‌فشانی چاره ندارم
verses.translit: Be gheyraz jân-feshâni châre nadâram
verses.english: And I have no choice except to sacrifice my life.'

poems.title: خداوندا در توفیق بگشای (O Lord, Open the Door of Success)
poems.translation: The opening sequence of Khosrow and Shirin, pleading for guidance.
poems.poetNotes: Nizami begs God to unlock his creative potential, grant him a heart filled with love, and keep his tongue pure to tell stories of beauty.
poems.culturalNotes: This prayer is a great example of the classical custom of 'Munajat' (intimate prayer) that starts epic poems.

verses.farsi: خداوندا در توفیق بگشای
verses.translit: Khodâvondâ dare toufigh begshây
verses.english: O Lord, open the door of success!

verses.farsi: نظامی را ره تحقیق بنمای
verses.translit: Nezâmi râ rahe tahghigh benmây
verses.english: Show Nizami the path of true verification.

verses.farsi: دلی ده کو ز خود بیزار باشد
verses.translit: Deli deh ku ze khod bizâr bâshad
verses.english: Grant me a heart that is weary of its own ego,

words.meaning: who / that he (contraction of که او)

verses.farsi: به عشق تو جهان‌افروز باشد
verses.translit: Be eshghe to jahân-afruz bâshad
verses.english: And which lights up the world with Your love.

verses.farsi: زبانم را به حمدت سازور کن
verses.translit: Zabânam râ be hamdat sâzvar kon
verses.english: Make my tongue harmonized with Your praise,

verses.farsi: روانم را ز نورت باخبر کن
verses.translit: Ravânam râ ze nurat bâ-khabar kon
verses.english: And make my soul aware of Your light.

words.meaning: my soul / my life / my walking

verses.farsi: در این دیوان که آغازش تو باشی
verses.translit: Dar in divân ke âghâzash to bâshi
verses.english: In this book of poetry which starts with You,

words.meaning: you are / you be (subjunctive singular of بودن)

verses.farsi: نخواهم جز ثنای تو تراشی
verses.translit: Nakhvâham joz sanâye to tarâshi
verses.english: I wish to carve out nothing but Your praise.

words.meaning: I will not want / I do not desire

verses.farsi: هدایت کن مرا در راه پاکی
verses.translit: Hedâyat kon marâ dar râhe pâki
verses.english: Guide me on the path of purity,

verses.farsi: رهایم کن ز بند درد و خاکی
verses.translit: Rahâyam kon ze bande dardo khâki
verses.english: And deliver me from the shackles of pain and dust.

poems.title: بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم (Opening of Makhzan al-Asrar)
poems.translation: The opening couplets of the Treasury of Secrets, discussing the key of speech.
poems.poetNotes: This is Nizami's most complex work. He opens with the Islamic formula, stating it acts as the key to the treasury of the divine Wise One.
poems.culturalNotes: The phrase 'کلید در گنج حکیم' (the key to the treasury of the Wise One) highlights Nizami's concept of poetry as an intellectual and spiritual treasure.

verses.farsi: بسم الله الرحمن الرحیم
verses.translit: Bismillâhir-rahmânir-rahim
verses.english: In the name of God, the Merciful, the Compassionate:

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: هست کلید در گنج حکیم
verses.translit: Hast kelide dare ganje hakim
verses.english: This is the key to the door of the Wise One's treasury.

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: فاتحه فکرت و ختم سخن
verses.translit: Fâteheye fekrat o khatme sokhan
verses.english: It is the opening of thought and the completion of speech;

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: اوست قدیم همه چرخ و کهن
verses.translit: Ust gadime hame charkh o kohan
verses.english: He is the ancient Lord of the universe and all that is old.

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: خالق افلاک و زمین و هوا
verses.translit: Khâleghe aflâko zamino havâ
verses.english: The Creator of the heavens, the earth, and the sky;

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: بخشنده نان به شاه و گدا
verses.translit: Bakhshandeye nân be shâho gadâ
verses.english: The Giver of daily bread to king and beggar alike.

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: در حریمش ز نام و نشان
verses.translit: Dar harimash ze nâmo neshân
verses.english: In His court of glory, far from name and sign,

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: عقل فرو مانده و جان سرکشان
verses.translit: Aghl foru mânde o jâne sarkeshân
verses.english: Intellect is left paralyzed, along with the souls of rebels.

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: پرده‌نشین همه رازهای ما
verses.translit: Parde-neshine hame râzhâye mâ
verses.english: He is the dweller behind the veil of all our secrets,

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

verses.farsi: همدم و همراه نیازهای ما
verses.translit: Hamdam o hamrâhe niyâzhâye mâ
verses.english: The companion and partner of all our needs.

phrases.meaning: treasury of the Wise One / God

poems.title: جهان عشق است و دیگر زرق‌سازی (The Philosophy of Love)
poems.translation: Nizami's philosophical statement on love as the cosmic motor of the universe.
poems.poetNotes: In this famous excerpt from Khosrow and Shirin, Nizami argues that love is the absolute foundation of reality. Without love, the skies would not rotate, and the earth would wither.
poems.culturalNotes: This text is a key reference in discussions of cosmic love in Persian literature, showing how romance is elevated to a structural principle of the universe.

verses.farsi: جهان عشق است و دیگر زرق‌سازی
verses.translit: Jahân eshgh ast o digar zargh-sâzi
verses.english: The world is love, and all else is mere deceit and tricks;

verses.farsi: به غیر از عشق هر کار است بازی
verses.translit: Be gheyraz eshgh har kâr ast bâzi
verses.english: Except for love, every other occupation is but a game.

verses.farsi: اگر بی‌عشق بودی چرخ گردون
verses.translit: Agar bi-eshgh budi charkhe gardun
verses.english: If the spinning wheel of heaven were without love,

verses.farsi: نگردیدی ز روی شوق افزون
verses.translit: Nagardidi ze ruye shogh afzun
verses.english: It would not rotate with ever-increasing passion.

verses.farsi: زمین بی‌عشق پژمرده و بی‌جان
verses.translit: Zamin bi-eshgh pejmorde va bi-jân
verses.english: The earth without love would be withered and lifeless;

verses.farsi: هوا بی‌عشق طوفانی و نالان
verses.translit: Havâ bi-eshgh tufâni va nâlân
verses.english: The wind without love would be stormy and wailing.

verses.farsi: بیا تا در سرای عشق باشیم
verses.translit: Biyâ tâ dar sarâye eshgh bâshim
verses.english: Come, let us dwell in the palace of love,

words.meaning: we be / let us be (subjunctive plural of بودن)

verses.farsi: ز بند هر غم و انده رها شیم
verses.translit: Ze bande har gham o andoh rahâ shimo
verses.english: And be delivered from the shackles of all grief and sorrow,

words.meaning: let us be (poetic contraction of شویم)

verses.farsi: که هر کو در طریق عشق جان داد
verses.translit: Ke har ku dar tarighe eshgh jân dâd
verses.english: For whoever gives their life on the path of love,

words.meaning: who / that he (contraction of که او)

verses.farsi: به عالم جاودانی و نشان داد
verses.translit: Be âlam jâvedâni va neshân dâd
verses.english: Bequeaths eternity and a shining mark to the world.

poems.title: هدیه (The Gift)
poems.translation: An intimate, melancholy modern poem pleading for light in the darkness.
poems.poetNotes: In 'The Gift', Forough Farrokhzad uses the simple, conversational language of modern blank verse to express vulnerability and the search for warmth in a cold world.
poems.culturalNotes: The line 'لطفا چراغ بیاور' (please bring a lamp) is famously used in modern Iranian art, films, and literature as a symbol of hope during dark times.

verses.farsi: من از نهایت شب حرف می‌زنم
verses.translit: Man az nahâyate shab harf mizanam
verses.english: I speak from the deep end of the night,

phrases.meaning: the extreme end of the night

verses.farsi: من از نهایت تاریکی
verses.translit: Man az nahâyate târiki
verses.english: I speak from the deep end of darkness,

phrases.meaning: the extreme end of the night

verses.farsi: و از نهایت شب حرف می‌زنم
verses.translit: Va az nahâyate shab harf mizanam
verses.english: And I speak from the deep end of the night.

phrases.meaning: the extreme end of the night

verses.farsi: اگر به خانه من آمدی برای من ای یار
verses.translit: Agar be khâneye man âmadi barâye man ey yâr
verses.english: If you come to my house, O beloved, for me

phrases.meaning: the extreme end of the night

verses.farsi: لطفا چراغ بیاور و یک دریچه که از آن
verses.translit: Lotfan cherâgh biyâvar o yek dariche ke az ân
verses.english: Please bring a lamp, and a small window through which

phrases.meaning: the extreme end of the night

verses.farsi: به ازدحام کوچه خوشبخت بنگرم
verses.translit: Be ezdehâme kucheye khoshbakht bangaram
verses.english: I may look at the crowd of the happy alleyway.

phrases.meaning: the extreme end of the night

poems.title: صدا، تنها صداست که می‌ماند (Only Voice Remains)
poems.translation: A powerful modern statement on the permanence of art and voice.
poems.poetNotes: This poem is a late-career masterpiece. Forough argues that while physical bodies decay and stars fall, the 'voice'—art, truth, and nature's calls—remains forever.
poems.culturalNotes: The title 'تنها صداست که می‌ماند' is an iconic phrase in Iran, representing the immortality of artistic expression.

verses.farsi: صدا، صدا، تنها صدا
verses.translit: Sedâ, sedâ, tanhâ sedâ
verses.english: Voice, voice, only voice!

verses.farsi: صدای خواهش شفاف آب به جاری شدن
verses.translit: Sedâye khâheshe shaffâfe âb be jâri shodan
verses.english: The sound of the water's clear plea to flow,

verses.farsi: صدای ریزش نور ستاره بر جدار خاک
verses.translit: Sedâye rizishe nure setâre bar jedâre khâk
verses.english: The sound of star-light falling upon the wall of the earth,

verses.farsi: صدای انعقاد نطفه پنهان عشق در رگ‌ها
verses.translit: Sedâye eneghâde notfeye penhâne eshgh dar raghâ
verses.english: The sound of the secret seed of love taking root in the veins,

verses.farsi: صدای وزش باد در پهنه وسیع دشت
verses.translit: Sedâye vazeshe bâd dar pahneye vasi'e dasht
verses.english: The sound of the wind blowing across the wide span of the plain.

verses.farsi: چرا توقف کنم، چرا؟
verses.translit: Cherâ tavaghghof konam, cherâ
verses.english: Why should I stop? Why?

verses.farsi: پرنده‌ها به جستجوی جانب آبی رفته‌اند
verses.translit: Parandehâ be jostojuye jânebe âbi rafte'and
verses.english: The birds have gone in search of the blue horizon.

verses.farsi: صداست که می‌ماند و تن فانیست
verses.translit: Sedâst ke mimânad o tan fâni'st
verses.english: It is the voice that remains, while the body is mortal;

verses.farsi: تنها صداست که می‌ماند
verses.translit: Tanhâ sedâst ke mimânad
verses.english: Only the voice remains.

poems.title: عروسک کوکی (The Wind-up Doll)
poems.translation: A biting critique of passive, traditional roles forced upon women.
poems.poetNotes: Using the metaphor of a wind-up toy doll, Forough criticizes the empty, routine social expectations that demand women be silent, domestic, and decorative.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a foundational text of Iranian feminist literature, using stark imagery to critique gender roles.

verses.farsi: بیش از اینها می‌توان خاموش ماند
verses.translit: Bish az inhâ mitavân khâmosh mând
verses.english: One can remain silent far more than this;

verses.farsi: می‌توان ساعات طولانی
verses.translit: Mitavân sâ'âte tulâni
verses.english: One can, for long hours,

verses.farsi: با نگاهی چون نگاه مردگان ثابت
verses.translit: Bâ negâhi chon negâhe mordegân sâbet
verses.english: With a fixed stare like the stare of the dead,

verses.farsi: خیره شد در دود یک سیگار
verses.translit: Khire shod dar dude yek sigâr
verses.english: Gaze into the smoke of a cigarette,

verses.farsi: خیره شد در شکل یک فنجان
verses.translit: Khire shod dar shekle yek fenjân
verses.english: Gaze into the shape of a cup.

verses.farsi: می‌توان با هر فشار پنجه‌ای بی‌قصد
verses.translit: Mitavân bâ har feshâre panje'i bi-ghasd
verses.english: One can, with any mindless pressure of a finger,

verses.farsi: شیون پر وحشتی را سر داد
verses.translit: Shiyane por vahshati râ sar dâd
verses.english: Release a scream of absolute horror.

verses.farsi: می‌توان چون عروسک‌های کوکی بود
verses.translit: Mitavân chon arousakhâye koki bud
verses.english: One can be like wind-up toy dolls,

verses.farsi: با دو چشم شیشه‌ای جهان را دید
verses.translit: Bâ do chashme shishe'i jahân râ did
verses.english: Viewing the world with two eyes of glass,

verses.farsi: در سبد خوابید و بیدار شد
verses.translit: Dar sabad khâbid o bidâr shod
verses.english: Sleeping and waking inside a felt basket.

poems.title: باد ما را خواهد برد (The Wind Will Carry Us)
poems.translation: A melancholic reflection on transience, autumn, and death.
poems.poetNotes: This poem is a dark, atmospheric reflection on mortality. Forough uses the sound of the wind in the trees as a reminder of the swift passage of time.
poems.culturalNotes: The title of this poem was famously used by the legendary Iranian director Abbas Kiarostami for his award-winning film of the same name.

verses.farsi: در شب کوچک من، افسوس
verses.translit: Dar shabe kucheke man, afsus
verses.english: In my tiny night, alas,

verses.farsi: باد با برگ درختان میعادی دارد
verses.translit: Bâd bâ barge derakhtân mi'âdi dârad
verses.english: The wind has a rendezvous with the leaves of the trees.

verses.farsi: در شب کوچک من دلهره ویرانیست
verses.translit: Dar shabe kucheke man delhoreye virâni'st
verses.english: In my tiny night, there is the anxiety of ruin.

verses.farsi: گوش کن، وزش باد را می‌شنوی؟
verses.translit: Gush kon, vazeshe bâd râ mishnavi
verses.english: Listen! Do you hear the blowing of the wind?

verses.farsi: من به این خوشبختی اندوهبار می‌نگرم
verses.translit: Man be in khoshbakhtiye andohbâr minegaram
verses.english: I look upon this melancholic happiness.

verses.farsi: من به این شب غریب معتادم
verses.translit: Man be in shabe gharib mo'tâdam
verses.english: I am addicted to this strange night.

verses.farsi: گوش کن، وزش باد را می‌شنوی؟
verses.translit: Gush kon, vazeshe bâd râ mishnavi
verses.english: Listen! Do you hear the blowing of the wind?

verses.farsi: باد در کوچه باغ‌ها می‌وزد
verses.translit: Bâd dar kuche bâgh-hâ mi-vazad
verses.english: The wind is blowing in the garden lanes.

verses.farsi: شب در عبور شتابناک خویش است
verses.translit: Shab dar obure shetâbnâke khish ast
verses.english: The night is in its fast, rushing passage.

verses.farsi: باد ما را خواهد برد، باد ما را خواهد برد
verses.translit: Bâd mâ râ khâhad bord, bâd mâ râ khâhad bord
verses.english: The wind will carry us away, the wind will carry us away.

poems.title: تولدی دیگر (Another Birth)
poems.translation: The opening lines of Forough's most celebrated collection of poetry.
poems.poetNotes: This poem represents Forough's absolute maturity as a poet. It defines life through simple, repetitive acts that are elevated to spiritual significance.
poems.culturalNotes: The title 'تولدی دیگر' represents the rebirth of modern Persian poetry, freeing it from the rigid formal constraints of classical meters.

verses.farsi: همه هستی من آیه تاریکیست
verses.translit: Hameye hastiye man âyeye târiki'st
verses.english: My entire existence is a dark verse,

verses.farsi: که تو را در خود تکرارکنان
verses.translit: Ke to râ dar khod tekrâr-konân
verses.english: Which, repeating you within itself,

verses.farsi: به سحرگاه شکفتن‌ها و رستن‌های ابدی خواهد برد
verses.translit: Be sahargâhe shekoftan-hâ va rostan-hâye abadi khâhad bord
verses.english: Will carry you to the dawn of eternal blossomings and growth.

verses.farsi: من در این آیه تو را آه کشیدم، آه
verses.translit: Man dar in âye to râ âh kashidam, âh
verses.english: In this dark verse, I sighed you, ah!

verses.farsi: من در این آیه تو را به درخت و آب پیوند زدم
verses.translit: Man dar in âye to râ be derakhto âb peyvand zadam
verses.english: In this verse, I grafted you onto the tree and the water.

verses.farsi: زندگی شاید یک خیابان دراز است
verses.translit: Zendegi shâyad yek khiyâbâne derâz ast
verses.english: Life is perhaps a long avenue

verses.farsi: که هر روز زنی با سبدی از آن می‌گذرد
verses.translit: Ke har ruz zani bâ sabadi az ân migozarad
verses.english: Through which a woman with a basket passes every day.

verses.farsi: زندگی شاید عبور گیج یک رهگذر است
verses.translit: Zendegi shâyad obure gije yek rahgozar ast
verses.english: Life is perhaps the dazed passage of a pedestrian

verses.farsi: که کلاه از سر برمی‌دارد
verses.translit: Ke kolâh az sar bar-midârad
verses.english: Who lifts his hat from his head,

verses.farsi: و به یک رهگذر دیگر با لبخندی بی‌معنی می‌گوید صبح بخیر
verses.translit: Va be yek rahgozare digar bâ labkhandi bi-ma'ni miguyad sobh bekheyr
verses.english: And says to another pedestrian with a meaningless smile: 'Good morning.'

poems.title: صدای پای آب (The Sound of Water's Footsteps)
poems.translation: The iconic opening lines of Sepehri's autobiographical poem of nature.
poems.poetNotes: Sohrab introduces himself as a painter-poet from Kashan, describing his simple life, his loving mother, and a close God who dwells in nature.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is the most famous modern blank verse poem in Iran. Its opening lines are universally recognized and quoted.

verses.farsi: اهل کاشانم
verses.translit: Ahle Kâshânam
verses.english: I am from Kashan;

verses.farsi: روزگارم بد نیست
verses.translit: Ruzegâram bad nist
verses.english: My life is not so bad.

verses.farsi: تکه نانی دارم، خرده هوشی، سر سوزن ذوقی
verses.translit: Tekke nâni dâram, khorde hushi, sare suzan zoughi
verses.english: I have a bite of bread, a scrap of intelligence, a tiny needle-point of taste.

verses.farsi: مادری دارم، بهتر از برگ درخت
verses.translit: Mâdari dâram, behtar az barge derakht
verses.english: I have a mother, better than the leaves of trees;

verses.farsi: دوستانی، بهتر از آب روان
verses.translit: Dustâni, behtar az âbe ravân
verses.english: And friends, better than flowing water.

verses.farsi: و خدایی که در این نزدیکیست
verses.translit: Va khodâ'i ke dar in nazdikist
verses.english: And a God who is nearby here,

verses.farsi: لای این شب‌بوها، پای آن کاج بلند
verses.translit: Lâye in shab-buhâ, pâye ân kâje boland
verses.english: Dwelling amidst these gillyflowers, beneath that tall pine,

verses.farsi: روی آگاهی آب، روی قانون گیاه
verses.translit: Ruye âgâhiye âb, ruye ghânune ghiyâh
verses.english: Upon the consciousness of water, upon the law of plants.

verses.farsi: من مسلمانم
verses.translit: Man mosalmânam
verses.english: I am a Muslim;

verses.farsi: قبله‌ام یک گل سرخ
verses.translit: Gheble'am yek gole sorkh
verses.english: My prayer direction is a red rose,

verses.farsi: جانمازم چشمه، مهرم نور
verses.translit: Jâ-namâzam cheshme, mehram nur
verses.english: My prayer-mat is a spring, my prayer-clay is light,

verses.farsi: دشت سجاده من
verses.translit: Dasht sajjâdeye man
verses.english: And the plain is my prayer carpet.

poems.title: نشانی (The Address)
poems.translation: A mystical modern quest for the home of the Friend.
poems.poetNotes: In 'The Address', Sepehri maps out a quest through nature's symbols to reach the 'home of the Friend'. It utilizes a conversational style to portray spiritual maturity.
poems.culturalNotes: The title 'خانه دوست کجاست' was famously used by the legendary director Abbas Kiarostami for his masterpiece film of the same name.

verses.farsi: خانه دوست کجاست؟
verses.translit: Khâneye dost kojâst
verses.english: 'Where is the Friend's home?'

verses.farsi: در فلق بود که پرسید سوار
verses.translit: Dar falagh bud ke porsid sovâr
verses.english: It was at dawn that the rider asked.

verses.farsi: آسمان مکثی کرد
verses.translit: Âsemân maksi kard
verses.english: The sky paused for a moment;

verses.farsi: رهگذر شاخه نوری که به لب داشت به تاریکی شن‌ها بخشید
verses.translit: Rahgozar shâkheye nuri ke be lab dâht be târikiye shenhâ bakhshid
verses.english: The pedestrian gifted the branch of light he held on his lips to the darkness of the sands,

verses.farsi: و به انگشت نشان داد و گفت:
verses.translit: Va be angosht neshân dâd o goft
verses.english: And pointed with his finger, saying:

verses.farsi: نرسیده به درخت،
verses.translit: Nareside be derakht
verses.english: 'Just before you reach the tree,

verses.farsi: کوچه‌باغی است که از خواب خدا سبزتر است
verses.translit: Kuche-bâghi'st ke az khâbe khodâ sabztar ast
verses.english: There is a garden lane that is greener than the dream of God.

verses.farsi: و در آن عشق به اندازه پرهای صداقت آبی است
verses.translit: Va dar ân eshgh be andâzeye parhâye sedâghat âbi'st
verses.english: And inside it, love is as blue as the feathers of honesty.

verses.farsi: می‌روی تا ته آن کوچه که از پشت بلوغ، سر به در می‌آرد
verses.translit: Miravi tâ tahe ân kuche ke az poshte bolugh sar be dar mi-ârad
verses.english: You walk to the end of that lane which emerges from behind maturity,

verses.farsi: پس به سمت گل تنهایی می‌پیچی
verses.translit: Pas be samte gole tanhâ'i mipichi
verses.english: Then you turn towards the flower of solitude.

verses.farsi: دو قدم مانده به گل،
verses.translit: Do ghadam mânde be gol
verses.english: Two steps before you reach the flower,

verses.farsi: پای فواره جاوید اساطیر زمین می‌مانی
verses.translit: Pâye favvâreye jâvide asâtire zamin mimâni
verses.english: You stop at the foot of the eternal fountain of earth's mythologies.'

poems.title: پشت کاج‌ها، باید کتاب را بست (Beyond the Pines)
poems.translation: An invitation to leave books behind and experience the physical world.
poems.poetNotes: In this modern poem, Sepehri advocates for direct sensory experience of nature over dry academic learning. He urges the reader to close their books and step into nature.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a great expression of Sohrab's Zen-like outlook, advocating for quiet awareness and living in the present moment.

verses.farsi: باید کتاب را بست
verses.translit: Bâyad ketâb râ bast
verses.english: One must close the book;

verses.farsi: باید بلند شد
verses.translit: Bâyad boland shod
verses.english: One must stand up,

verses.farsi: در امتداد وقت قدم زد
verses.translit: Dar emtedâde vaght ghadam zad
verses.english: Walk along the span of time,

verses.farsi: گل را نگاه کرد
verses.translit: Gol râ negâh kard
verses.english: Gaze upon the flower,

verses.farsi: ابهام را شنید
verses.translit: Ebhâm râ shenid
verses.english: Hear the ambiguity.

verses.farsi: باید دوید تا بن بستی
verses.translit: Bâyad david tâ bon-basti
verses.english: One must run until reaching a dead end;

words.meaning: you closed / you tied (past singular of بستن)

verses.farsi: باید نشست نزدیک چشمه‌ها
verses.translit: Bâyad neshast nazdike cheshmehâ
verses.english: One must sit beside the water springs,

verses.farsi: و تر شد از طراوت پاک زمین
verses.translit: Va tar shod az tarâvate pâke zamin
verses.english: And become wet with the clean freshness of the earth.

verses.farsi: باید ببوئیم یاس را
verses.translit: Bâyad bebu'im yâs râ
verses.english: We must smell the jasmine,

verses.farsi: و عشق بورزیم به برگ درختان
verses.translit: Va eshgh varzim be barge derakhtân
verses.english: And show love to the leaves of the trees.

words.meaning: we practice / show (subjunctive plural of ورزیدن)

poems.title: قایقی خواهم ساخت (I Will Build a Boat)
poems.translation: A beautiful modern poem of escaping a cold society for a land of dreams.
poems.poetNotes: This poem is a modern classic. Sepehri vows to build a boat to leave 'this strange land' where no one values love, sailing away to a legendary city beyond the seas.
poems.culturalNotes: The phrase 'پشت دریاها شهریست' (beyond the seas, there is a city) is an iconic symbol of utopian hope in modern Iranian culture.

verses.farsi: قایقی خواهم ساخت
verses.translit: Ghayeghi khâham sâkht
verses.english: I will build a boat,

verses.farsi: خواهم انداخت به آب
verses.translit: Khâham andâkht be âb
verses.english: I will cast it onto the water,

verses.farsi: دور خواهم شد از این خاک غریب
verses.translit: Dur khâham shod az in khâke gharib
verses.english: I will distance myself from this strange soil

verses.farsi: که در آن هیچ‌کسی نیست که در بیشه عشق
verses.translit: Ke dar ân hich-kasi nist ke dar bisheye eshgh
verses.english: In which there is no one left, who inside the forest of love,

verses.farsi: قهرمانان را بیدار کند
verses.translit: Ghahremânân râ bidâr konad
verses.english: Would wake up the sleeping heroes.

verses.farsi: قایق از تور هنوز
verses.translit: Ghayegh az tur hanuz
verses.english: The boat is still empty of nets,

verses.farsi: و دل از شوق رهایی لرزان
verses.translit: Va del az shoghe rahâ'i larzân
verses.english: And the heart is trembling from the passion of freedom.

verses.farsi: همچنان خواهم راند
verses.translit: Hamchonân khâham rând
verses.english: I will continue to sail on.

verses.farsi: پشت دریاها شهریست
verses.translit: Poshte daryâhâ shahri'st
verses.english: Beyond the seas, there is a city,

verses.farsi: که در آن پنجره‌ها رو به تجلی باز است
verses.translit: Ke dar ân panjerehâ ru be tajalli bâz ast
verses.english: Where the windows are open towards divine illumination.

poems.title: آب را گل نکنیم (Let's Not Muddy the Water)
poems.translation: An eco-mystical modern poem promoting empathy for downstream life.
poems.poetNotes: In this poem, Sepehri advocates for deep environmental empathy. He asks us not to muddy the stream, as a bird, a traveler, or a beautiful woman downstream might be using it.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a key educational text in Iran, teaching children environmental responsibility and basic respect for fellow humans.

poems.title: سیر و پیاز (The Garlic and the Onion)
poems.translation: A humorous dialogue fable criticking people who highlight others' flaws.
poems.poetNotes: Parvin Etesami uses a debate between garlic and an onion. The garlic mocks the onion's bad smell, and the onion replies that garlic should look at its own severe odor.
poems.culturalNotes: This fable is a popular ethical story in Iran, encouraging self-reflection and warning against arrogance and hypocrisy.

poems.title: مست و هوشیار (The Drunk and the Constable)
poems.translation: A sharp, satirical debate exposing institutional and religious hypocrisy.
poems.poetNotes: This is Parvin's most famous poem. It is a debate between an intoxicated citizen and a corrupt city constable (Muhtasib), exposing that the judge and leaders are far more corrupt than the drunkard.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a classic of modern political satire, frequently cited in discussions of justice, law, and moral hypocrisy in Iran.

poems.title: اشک یتیم (Tears of the Orphan)
poems.translation: A powerful critique of royal opulence at the expense of the poor.
poems.poetNotes: In this poem, a king passes by a crowd. A child asks what the sparkling gem on the crown is, and an elder answers that it is actually the blood and tears of the poor.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a classic of Iranian social realism, highlighting Parvin's deep commitment to the struggle of the lower classes.

verses.english: An orphan child asked from midst the crowd:

verses.farsi: کاین تابناک چیست که بر تاج پادشاست
verses.translit: K'in tâbnâk chist ke bar tâje pâdeshâst
verses.english: 'What is that sparkling object upon the king's crown?'

poems.title: گفت سوزن با نخ کای هرزه‌پوی (The Needle and the Thread)
poems.translation: An educational dialogue promoting cooperation and mutual dependency.
poems.poetNotes: Using a debate between a needle and a thread, Parvin illustrates that individual talent is useless without cooperation. The needle path-clears, but the thread holds the cloth together.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem is a common fable taught in Iran to explain teamwork and the value of ordinary labor.

poems.title: روزی زنی به همسر خود گفت در سخن (The Tear and the Jewel)
poems.translation: A moral debate on emotional authenticity vs material wealth.
poems.poetNotes: This debate contrasts a costly jewel on a king's crown with a tear shed by a poor widow. Parvin asserts that the tear of the suffering is far more precious in the eyes of truth.
poems.culturalNotes: This poem showcases Parvin's characteristic use of everyday objects to convey high ethical values.
